result.text:
above it.                                                                                                                                                                                             █
 - After a heading: zero or one blank line. The heading should sit close to the content it introduces, so the reader immediately associates the two.                                                   █
 - Between paragraphs: one blank line. This is the baseline rhythm — enough to separate ideas without creating large gaps that waste vertical space.                                                   █
 - Before a code block: one blank line, so the code container has breathing room from the prose that introduces it.                                                                                    █
 - After a code block: one blank line, so the following paragraph or heading does not collide with the block's bottom border.                                                                          █
 - Before a table: one blank line, matching the code-block rule.                                                                                                                                       █
 - After a table: one blank line, same reason.                                                                                                                                                         █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 The key principle is consistency: the blank-line count between block types should follow a predictable pattern so the document has an even cadence. If a renderer inserts two blank lines before one  █
 heading but only one before the next, the reader perceives the document as uneven, even if they cannot articulate why.                                                                                █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 A common mistake is collapsing consecutive blank lines in the source markdown down to zero. The renderer should treat a single blank line in the source as a single blank line in the output, but it  █
 should also enforce its own minimum spacing rules regardless of source — so even if the source has no blank line before a heading, the renderer still inserts one, because the visual hierarchy       █
 depends on it.                                                                                                                                                                                        █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Is there a specific aspect of the spacing rhythm you want me to dig into further, or is this connected to a rendering issue you're seeing in Rika?                                                    █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 ┃ Second queued follow-up prompt about table borders                                                                                                                                                  █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Table borders in a terminal markdown renderer serve two purposes at once: they visually contain the grid so a reader can scan rows and columns quickly, and they enforce structural alignment so      █
 cell content never bleeds across column boundaries.                                                                                                                                                   █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 The main decisions a renderer has to make:                                                                                                                                                            █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Border style. Most TUI renderers pick from a small set of box-drawing character sets:                                                                                                                 █
 - Light (─, │, ┌, ┐, └, ┘, ├, ┤, ┬, ┴, ┼) — thin single-line borders, the most common choice.                                                                                                         █
 - Rounded (╭, ╮, ╰, ╯ for corners, with light edges and junctions) — visually softer, often used when the rest of the UI uses rounded frames.                                                         █
 - Heavy (━, ┃, ┏, etc.) — thick borders, rarely used for markdown tables because they feel heavy against prose.                                                                                       █
 - Double (═, ║, ╔, etc.) — even rarer, tends to look ornate.                                                                                                                                          █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 The renderer picks one style and uses it consistently for every table in the document. Mixing styles within a single document breaks the visual cadence.                                              █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Header separator. The markdown source contains a delimiter row (| --- | --- |), and the renderer turns that into a horizontal rule between the header and the first data row. This rule uses the      █
 same border character weight as the outer frame but is drawn as a full-width line with junction characters where column separators meet it (├─┬─┤ for the top of a light border, or the rounded       █
 equivalents). The header separator is the single most important border element because it tells the reader where labels end and data begins.                                                          █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Column separators. Inside the grid, each column boundary is drawn as a vertical line running from the top border to the bottom border. These separators are what let a reader's eye track across a    █
 wide row and match a cell to its header. Without them, a table with long wrapped cells becomes indistinguishable from indented prose. The separators must connect cleanly to the top border, the      █
 header-separator rule, and the bottom border using the correct junction characters — a missing or mismatched junction immediately looks broken.                                                       █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Background and color. Some renderers fill the cell background with a subtle shade to distinguish the table from surrounding prose. Others leave the terminal background visible and rely on the       █
 border alone. When a renderer does paint a background, it must respect the terminal's own background color — painting a hard dark fill on a light terminal (or vice versa) creates a jarring          █
 rectangle that breaks the document's flow. Background inheritance, where the table's fill matches the terminal's native background, is the safest approach for cross-theme compatibility.             █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Border integrity under wrapping. When a cell's content is longer than the column width allows, the renderer soft-wraps the cell text onto additional lines. The border characters on the left and     █
 right of that cell must repeat or extend for every wrapped line — the vertical separator cannot stop at the first line and leave the wrapped continuation unbounded. The bottom border of the table   █
 is drawn only once, after the tallest row in the grid, so every column ends at the same horizontal line even if one column has far more wrapped content than another.                                 █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Width budget. The renderer measures the terminal width, subtracts the space consumed by border characters and padding (typically one space on each side of every cell), and distributes the           █
 remaining width across columns. The simplest strategy gives each column its natural width up to the available budget and wraps only the columns that overflow. More sophisticated renderers compute   █
 a weighted distribution so that a table with one narrow column and one wide column does not waste space on the narrow one while aggressively wrapping the wide one.                                   █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 Is there a specific border behavior you want to compare between Rika and Amp, or is this about deciding which border style Rika should use?                                                           █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 ┃ Think carefully and silently for a while, then in one sentence name your favorite terminal border style.                                                                                            █
                                                                                                                                                                                                       █
 My favorite is the rounded border style (╭╮╰╯ with light edges) because the softened corners feel less mechanical than sharp right angles while still maintaining clear structural containment,       █
 which makes tables and code blocks sit more                                                                                                                                                           █
 ╭────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
